refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation communication packet loss at or below the customer-defined threshold (acceptance criterion: ≤1% under agreed network conditions) to ensure safe and effective remote equipment operation.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-009 addresses a critical quality-of-service requirement for teleoperation. Packet loss directly impacts command latency, feedback fidelity, and operator situational awareness—all safety-critical factors in remote heavy equipment control. Refinement clarifies that the requirement is conditional on 'agreed network conditions' and ties acceptance to a measurable, customer-negotiated target rather than a fixed value.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Conduct packet loss measurement under simulated and live network conditions (WiFi, cellular, hybrid) matching customer deployment scenarios. Measure end-to-end loss across the teleoperation link (operator console → vehicle → operator console). Acceptance: measured packet loss ≤ customer-defined limit for ≥95% of test runs.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-009.1: The teleoperation system shall implement error detection and recovery mechanisms to minimize packet loss impact on command execution.
FR-NGHE-009.2: The teleoperation system shall select and configure network protocols and codecs to achieve the target packet loss limit under agreed conditions.
FR-NGHE-009.3: The teleoperation system shall monitor and report real-time packet loss metrics to the operator and safety services.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement adaptive bitrate streaming with forward error correction (FEC) and automatic codec switching based on network quality metrics.
DP-B: Deploy redundant communication channels (primary + backup) with automatic failover to maintain packet loss below threshold.
DP-C: Use application-level acknowledgment and selective retransmission of critical telemetry and command packets.
DP-D: Implement a hybrid approach combining FEC, redundant channels, and selective retransmission with real-time quality monitoring.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-D: Implement a hybrid approach combining forward error correction (FEC), redundant communication channels (primary + backup with automatic failover), and selective retransmission of safety-critical packets, coupled with real-time packet loss monitoring and operator alerting.

potential_sources: DP-D couples teleoperation protocol design (FEC/retransmission logic) with network infrastructure selection (redundant channels) and safety alerting (operator notification). These are logically distinct but operationally interdependent.

Risk: If network redundancy is not available in customer environment, FEC and retransmission alone may be insufficient. Mitigation: Modular design allows graceful degradation; FEC + retransmission can operate independently of redundancy.
coupling_check.notes: Coupling is acceptable because all three mechanisms (FEC, redundancy, retransmission) are necessary to meet the high safety criticality constraint. Decoupling would require separate DPs for each mechanism, but they must be coordinated at the teleoperation module level. The hybrid approach is justified by the safety-critical nature of the requirement.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Customer will define and agree on 'network conditions' (bandwidth, latency, interference profile) before implementation.
Assumption: Vehicle and operator console hardware support the computational overhead of FEC encoding/decoding and dual-channel management.
Risk: FEC overhead increases bandwidth consumption; if network is severely constrained, FEC may paradoxically increase packet loss. Mitigation: Adaptive FEC tuning based on real-time metrics.
Risk: Redundant channels add cost and complexity; customer may not accept this trade-off. Mitigation: Offer tiered solutions (FEC-only, FEC+redundancy, full hybrid).

Risk: Selective retransmission logic must distinguish safety-critical packets (e.g., emergency stop) from non-critical telemetry; incorrect classification could degrade safety. Mitigation: Explicit packet tagging and validation.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-D to explicitly specify: (1) FEC scheme (e.g., Reed-Solomon, Turbo codes) and overhead budget; (2) redundancy topology (e.g., LTE + WiFi, or dual LTE carriers); (3) retransmission timeout and retry limits for critical vs. non-critical packets; (4) monitoring thresholds and operator alert triggers. This will reduce ambiguity and enable clearer IM assignment.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: The Teleoperation module owns the end-to-end teleoperation communication stack, including protocol implementation (FEC, redundancy management, selective retransmission), real-time packet loss monitoring, and operator alerting. It interfaces with the Vehicle module for command/telemetry exchange and with Safety Services for safety-critical alerts.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module sends operator commands (movement, tool control, mode changes) to Vehicle via Tasking interface.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module receives vehicle state, sensor data, and operational metrics from Vehicle via Telemetry interface.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module sends packet loss warnings and communication quality alerts to Safety Services via SafetyAlert interface.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module manages primary and backup network connections (WiFi, cellular, hybrid) via Network Link interface for redundancy and failover.

module_containment_scores.notes: Teleoperation is the natural owner of packet loss mitigation logic. FEC, redundancy, and retransmission are core teleoperation concerns. Minimal diffusion: only external interfaces to Vehicle (Tasking/Telemetry), Safety Services (SafetyAlert), and Network Link (redundancy management).

Vehicle module should not own teleoperation packet loss logic; it is a consumer of commands and producer of telemetry. However, Vehicle may need to implement command validation and acknowledgment to support selective retransmission. This is a secondary concern and should be delegated to Teleoperation.

Safety Services should monitor and respond to packet loss alerts but should not own the packet loss mitigation mechanisms themselves. Safety Services is a consumer of SafetyAlert events from Teleoperation.
NGHE Solution is the system-level container but should not directly implement packet loss mitigation. This is a subsystem-level concern owned by Teleoperation.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the optimal owner of DP-D (hybrid packet loss mitigation). It has clear ownership of the teleoperation communication stack, direct interfaces to Vehicle (Tasking/Telemetry) and Safety Services (SafetyAlert), and can manage network redundancy via the Network Link interface without diffusion to other modules.
containment_risks: Risk: If Vehicle module lacks command acknowledgment capability, Teleoperation cannot implement selective retransmission effectively. Mitigation: Ensure Vehicle module supports acknowledgment frames for critical commands.
Risk: Network Link interface may not support dual-channel management (e.g., simultaneous WiFi + cellular). Mitigation: Clarify Network Link interface specification to include redundancy primitives.
Risk: Safety Services may not be ready to consume SafetyAlert events at the required latency. Mitigation: Define SafetyAlert interface SLA (e.g., <100ms latency for critical alerts).
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Confirm that the Network Link interface supports dual-channel management and failover primitives. If not, either extend Network Link or introduce a new 'Network Redundancy Manager' sub-module within Teleoperation to abstract redundancy logic. Additionally, ensure Vehicle module supports command acknowledgment for critical packets (e.g., emergency stop).

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-D (hybrid FEC + redundancy + selective retransmission) directly addresses FR-NGHE-009 (packet loss ≤ customer-defined threshold). The DP is comprehensive, accounts for high safety criticality, and provides multiple mitigation mechanisms to handle diverse network conditions. No logical gaps detected.

diffusion_risks: Moderate risk: Redundancy management (dual-channel failover) may require coordination with Network Link interface. If Network Link does not natively support redundancy, Teleoperation must implement failover logic, increasing complexity.
Low risk: Selective retransmission requires Vehicle module to support command acknowledgment. If Vehicle lacks this, Teleoperation must infer packet loss via timeout, reducing efficiency.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module is well-contained for DP-D. All core logic (FEC, retransmission, monitoring) resides within Teleoperation. External dependencies (Vehicle acknowledgment, Network Link redundancy, Safety Services alerting) are minimal and well-defined via interfaces. No significant diffusion detected.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification is feasible. Packet loss can be measured end-to-end via network simulation (e.g., tc/netem on Linux) or live field testing. Acceptance criterion (≤1% packet loss under agreed conditions) is measurable and testable.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear. Teleoperation module owns packet loss mitigation. Vehicle module owns command acknowledgment (secondary). Safety Services owns alert consumption (secondary). Network Link owns redundancy primitives (secondary). No ambiguity in responsibility.

recommended_revisions: Clarify Network Link interface specification to explicitly support dual-channel management and automatic failover.
Define SafetyAlert interface SLA for packet loss warnings (e.g., latency, frequency, severity levels).
Specify Vehicle module's command acknowledgment protocol (e.g., ACK frame format, timeout behavior).
Document FEC scheme selection criteria and overhead budget (e.g., max 20% bandwidth overhead).

decision.justification: FR-NGHE-009 is not atomic and has been decomposed into three sub-FRs (error detection/recovery, protocol/codec selection, monitoring/reporting). Each sub-FR requires further refinement and DP selection before implementation. The current level (FR-NGHE-009 + DP-D + Teleoperation) is strategically sound and passes all gates, but tactical details (FEC scheme, redundancy topology, retransmission logic) must be elaborated at the next decomposition level.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-009.1: The teleoperation system shall implement error detection and recovery mechanisms to minimize packet loss impact on command execution.
FR-NGHE-009.2: The teleoperation system shall select and configure network protocols and codecs to achieve the target packet loss limit under agreed conditions.
FR-NGHE-009.3: The teleoperation system shall monitor and report real-time packet loss metrics to the operator and safety services.

assumptions: Customer will define and agree on 'network conditions' (bandwidth, latency, interference profile) before implementation.
Vehicle module supports command acknowledgment for critical packets.
Network Link interface supports dual-channel management and automatic failover.
Safety Services is ready to consume SafetyAlert events at required latency (<100ms for critical alerts).
Operator console and vehicle hardware have sufficient computational capacity for FEC encoding/decoding and dual-channel management.
Packet loss measurement methodology is agreed upon (e.g., end-to-end, per-link, or application-level).
open_risks: Network Link interface may not natively support redundancy; Teleoperation may need to implement failover logic, increasing complexity.
FEC overhead could paradoxically increase packet loss if network is severely constrained; adaptive tuning required.
Selective retransmission logic must correctly classify safety-critical vs. non-critical packets; misclassification could degrade safety.
Customer may not accept cost/complexity of redundant channels; tiered solutions may be needed.
Real-time monitoring and alerting may introduce latency that conflicts with safety requirements; SLA definition needed.
evidence_needed: Network Link interface specification (redundancy primitives, failover behavior).
Vehicle module command acknowledgment protocol specification.
SafetyAlert interface SLA (latency, frequency, severity levels).
FEC scheme selection rationale and overhead budget.
Redundancy topology options and cost/benefit analysis.
Packet loss measurement methodology and test plan.
Customer agreement on 'network conditions' and acceptance criteria.
Operator console and vehicle hardware capability assessment (computational capacity, memory, power).
